A hypothesis is presented to explain some of the dramatic differences in reactor operating characteristics between the $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ and $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ runs.

The liquidus temperature ranges from 800 to $940^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , depending on the exact carrier salt composition, with the nominal reactor operating temperature being $\sim1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
A 7.3-MW(t) experimental reactor based on this concept was built and operated at Oak Ridge National Laboratory.
This reactor, the Molten-Salt Reactor Experiment (hereafter called the MSRE), first went critical in June 1965.
Nuclear operations were terminated in December 1969.
This information is critical in the design of large central power stations [1000 MW(e)] where the heat generated by fission products is substantial.

Fission products in molten fuel salt can be grouped into three principal types where the mechanics of migration is the distinguishing feature - (1) salt seekers, (2) noble gases, and (3) noble metals.
deal of work has been done to understand noble gas migration, particularly $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ because of its thermal neutron cross section of over $10^{6}$ barns.
It will also be used to suggest an explanation for the rather dramatic difference in reactor operating characteristics between runs made with $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ and $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ fuels.
The operating power level was 7.3 MW(t).
Its composition and physical properties are given in Table 2.1.
Composition - LiF, BeF2, ZrF4, UF4 (65.0, 29.1, 5.0, 0.9 mole %) Liquid type - Newtonian

The nominal operating temperature was $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ with a $40^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ temperature change across the core and primary heat exchanger.
Figure 2.1 is a schematic flow diagram of the MSRE, which will be described briefly here.
The nominal flow rate was $1200\mathrm{gpm}$ .

section: Figure 2.1. Schematic Flow Diagram Of The Msre
text: The MSRE went critical June 1, 1965, and nuclear operations were terminated December 12, 1969.

section: Figure 2.1. Schematic Flow Diagram Of The Msre
text: The reactor was critical for a total of 17,655 hr.

section: Figure 2.1. Schematic Flow Diagram Of The Msre
text: Figure 2.2 is a brief historical outline of the MSRE's power operation.

section: Figure 2.1. Schematic Flow Diagram Of The Msre
text: Note particularly that for the first 2 l/2 years the reactor was operated with $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ fuel.

section: Figure 2.1. Schematic Flow Diagram Of The Msre
text: The fuel was chemically processed and the $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ replaced with $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .

section: Figure 2.1. Schematic Flow Diagram Of The Msre
text: Then for the last 1 l/2 years, the reactor was operated with $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ fuel.

section: Figure 2.1. Schematic Flow Diagram Of The Msre
text: Most of the results presented in this report will be from data obtained during the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ runs.
A detailed view of the MSRE core and reactor vessel is shown in Figure 2.3.
The moderator assembly was composed of graphite stringers about 5 ft long and 2 in. square.
In the bulk of the fuel channels (95 percent of them), the fuel salt velocity was about 0.7 ft/sec, yielding a Reynolds number of about 1000.
Details of the specimen holder are shown in Figure 2.4.
A detail drawing of the pump is shown in Figure 2.5.
Its rated capacity is $1200\mathrm{gpm}$ of fuel salt at 48.5 ft of head.
It also contained the $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ stripping system, a salt level indicator, and the fuel salt sampling facility.
The normal operating helium pressure in the pump bowl, which was also the pump suction pressure, was about 5 psig.
A toroidal spray ring containing many small holes (146 - 1/8 in. holes, and 145 - 1/16 in. holes), sprayed the salt through the gas phase.
The salt flow, which resulted in a mean jet velocity of 7.2 ft/sec, was estimated (not measured) to be at a rate of about 50 gpm.

The xenon rich cover gas was purged from the pump bowl by a continuous flow of clean helium from (1) the pump shaft purge, (2) two bubbler level indicators, and (3) the reference pressure line purge for the bubblers.

section: Figure 2.5. Msre Fuel Pump And Overflow Tank
text: The general conclusion from these analyses is that the volume fraction of circulating bubbles in the fuel loop during the $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ runs was 0.0002 to 0.00045, $^{(4,5)}$ and the void fraction during the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ runs was 0.005 and 0.006. $^{(4)}$ A hypothesis is presented in Section 3.3 to explain this rather large change in circulating void fraction between the $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ and $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ runs.

text: This sampling facility was used for a variety of purposes including (1) taking fuel salt samples, (2) taking gas phase samples, (3) adding uranium to the fuel salt, (4) adding chemicals to the fuel salt to control its oxidation-reduction state, and (5) exposing materials to fuel salt for short periods of time.

We will also discuss the rather dramatic differences in the reactor operating characteristics when fueled with $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ and $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ , and propose a reason for this difference.
For instance $^{95}\mathrm{Zr}$ has a yield of about 6.2 percent but its precursor, $^{95}\mathrm{Kr}$ , has a yield of only 0.007 percent and is very short lived.
Therefore any migrational tendency of the $^{95}\mathrm{Kr}$ will have little effect on the $^{95}\mathrm{Zr}$ .
Indeed the complete $^{95}\mathrm{Zr}$ inventory was found in the fuel salt.(6) As opposed to this, consider the salt seeker $^{137}\mathrm{Cs}$ with a cumulative yield of 6.15 percent.
Most of its yield comes from the decay of $^{137}\mathrm{Xe}$ that has a yield of about 6.0 percent and a half life of 3.9 min.

Accordingly, only 80 to 90 percent of the $^{137}\mathrm{Cs}$ inventory was found in the fuel salt(6) and significant quantities were found deep inside the graphite where it deposited upon decay of its precursor.\* (7,8) In conclusion, the salt seeking fission products are well behaved and remain dissolved in the circulating fuel salt in inventory quantities except when influenced by the behavior of their precursors.
cross section of $3 \times 10^{6}$ barns.
Because of $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ , a great deal of work has gone into quantitatively understanding noble gas migration in the MSRE.

text: I do not intend to suggest an increase in bubble stability in the pump bowl anything like this, rather that the increase in bubble stability between the $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ and $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ runs was relatively minor.
The bubble half life would be 0.693/λ.
A mechanism like this, with an increase in bubble lifetime of this magnitude, could easily account for the higher overflow rates experienced during the $^{233}U$ runs.

This, then is a description of the differences in operating characteristics between the $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ and $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ runs in the MSRE, and a suggested hypothesis to explain the differences.

We must do this primarily because of $^{95}\mathrm{Nb}$ whose precursor is $^{95}\mathrm{Zr}$ with a half life of 65 days.

The model will consider the entire fuel loop to be a "well stirred pot." One might expect this assumption to be adequate because the fuel circuit time around the loop is about 25 seconds and the isotopes we will be dealing with have half lives ranging from hours to years.

If we assume that noble metals deposit on liquid-gas interfaces in accordance with the mass transfer theory and that the sticking fraction is 1.0, we again arrive at a similar equation for the rate of migration to bubbles.

text: MEASURED AMOUNT OF GRAPHITE SPECIMEN $(\mathrm{dpm} / \mathrm{cm}^2)$ THEORETICAL AMOUNT ON GRAPHITE SPECIMEN $(\mathrm{dpm} / \mathrm{cm}^2)$
Three different sets of graphite samples (Figure 5.2) were exposed to fuel salt during only the $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ operations.
An exception is $^{95}\mathrm{Nb}$ which lies well above the line.
Recall that the fuel salt was in a reduced state during the $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ operations and $^{95}\mathrm{Nb}$ behaved as a noble metal and would therefore be expected to fall on the line.
The slope seems to be zero however for half lives over about 500 hours.
noted that this is likely in observation number 6 above.
This will be confirmed more directly in Section 5.7 when we look at the results of a special laminar flow core surveillance test during the last MSRE operational period.
Now, there is evidence $^{(30)}$ that Nb forms a stable carbide with graphite under MSRE operating conditions.

An effective sticking fraction of $^{95}\mathrm{Nb}$ to graphite of unity, and a sticking fraction of the other noble metals of less than unity, would explain the $^{95}\mathrm{Nb}$ points being so much higher than the other noble metals in Figure 5.2.
In fact, the ratio between the curves and the $^{95}\mathrm{Nb}$ data cluster could be a direct measure of the sticking fraction of the other noble metals relative to $^{95}\mathrm{Nb}$ .
We can carry this discussion one step further. $^{99}\mathrm{Nb}$ (half life = 2.4 min) is a precursor of $^{99}\mathrm{Mo}$ .

If then, amounts of $^{99}\mathrm{Nb}$ migrate to the graphite with a sticking fraction of unity and decay into $^{99}\mathrm{Mo}$ , the measured amounts of $^{99}\mathrm{Mo}$ would also be relatively higher than the other noble metals.
Therefore, the left side of the curves in Figure 5.2, which are controlled to an extent by $^{99}\mathrm{Mo}$ , might tend to be higher than the other noble metals.
Another effect that must be considered is that during the final week of run 18, the fuel pump was operated at a slightly reduced speed.
The principal effect was that during this period the circulating void fraction was more equivalent to the $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ runs than the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ runs.
This last point may also help explain why the short half life end of the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ curve for deposition on Hastelloy-N (Figure 5.3) is higher than expected.

The conclusions then, from examination of noble metal deposition data from the core surveillance samples, is that they generally confirm the conclusions arrived at after examination of the heat exchanger in Section 5.2.

text: The back calculation in time is sensitive and a small error in the $^{95}\mathrm{Zr}$ concentration measurement
can result in a negative $^{95}\mathrm{Nb}$ concentration.

The shotgun pattern and also the disappearance and reappearance of $^{95}\mathrm{Nb}$ in Figure 5.9 is apparently related in some way to the fuel salt fluctuating between the oxidized and reduced state.
Particularly the shorter lived isotopes $(^{99}\mathrm{Mo}$ and $^{132}\mathrm{Te})$ almost seem to be approaching a value of unity (measured concentration = theoretical concentration).

Let us only say that if we did include this term in the calculation, then the data points in Figures 5.4 - 5.8 could rise more or less uniformly by as much as a factor of 5 during the $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ operation (stick fraction to bubbles = 1.0) and a good deal more for the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ operation.
Many qualitative observations discussed in section 3 also confirm this hypothesis.

This suggestion has been made before.(31, 32) It has been suggested in Section 3.3 of this report that noble corrosion products and fission products accumulating in the pump bowl and displaying the properties of insoluble surface active agents are responsible for the differences between the $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ and $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ operating characteristics of the MSRE.

As a matter of fact, flotation of reduced Fe, Ni and Cr from fuel salt has been demonstrated in a test tube scale laboratory experiment.(32) Froth flotation is a common unit operation in the ore processing industries.
The fundamental requirements for a flotation process to work are:(33)
Contact angles are reported in the range of $90 - 105^{\circ}$ .
(17) They are mostly unwet and in a range for good flotation.
As noted in Section 3.3, the property of surfactants that is significant to this analysis is that they enhance bubble stability and also result in smaller bubbles being generated in the pump bowl.
This then is the suggested explanation for the curves in Figures 5.4 - 5.8.
2, are still not representative of the fuel salt.
5, the concentrations of all noble metal isotopes are usually consistent in an individual sample (either high or low).
The resulting correlation is shown in Figure 5.10 and represents a plot similar to that used in analysis of noble metals on the heat exchanger and core surveillance samples.
Recall that the equivalent plots for the heat exchanger and core surveillance sample yielded a line with a slope of zero, whereas the slopes of the lines in Figure 5.10 are quite high.
In Section 5.6 of this report we will attach some physical significance to the slope of these lines.
5.5 Comparison of Gas Samples with $C^S$
In Section 5.2 it was noted that noble metal atoms or very small clusters of them can be spontaneously expelled from the surface of quiescent fuel salt.
The analytical model developed in Section 4 does not contain a term for the noble metal concentration in the gas phases.
Figure 5.12 shows the results of this calculation.
Figure 5.13 is a plot of gas sample data equivalent to Figure 5.11 for salt samples.
All the reported data from routine gas samples taken during each $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ run were geometrically averaged and plotted against the noble metal half life.
As noted before this behavior is particularly striking when these curves are compared with the curves in Figure 5.11 for salt samples.
Recall that at the start of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ operation, a large amount of reduced corrosion products (which act as noble metals) suddenly appeared in the reactor system.

Following Run 18, the regular core surveillance specimen fixture (Fig.
2.4) was removed and a special test fixture was installed.
One of the elements that was devoted to fission product deposition is illustrated in Figure 5.15.
This sample fixture was removed after Run 20 when the MSRE was permanently shut down, and the noble metal deposition was measured.
Theoretical amounts of noble metals were computed and compared to the measured amounts in Figure 5.16.
The estimated salt velocity through the annulus is 0.7 ft/sec.
This will yield a Reynolds number of 206 and a mean mass transfer coefficient throughout the annulus of about 0.23 ft/hr.
The data points on Figure 5.16 represent the geometric averages of all determinations on the Hastelloy-N and graphite annular surfaces.
The curves in Figure 5.16 are generally consistent in magnitude of the ordinate with those from the primary heat exchanger (Fig.
5.1) and the Hastelloy-N and graphite core surveillance specimens (Figures 5.3 and 5.2, respectively).
Because of scatter in the data, it is difficult to draw a firm value, but something in the range of 0.1 - 0.6 would be in order.

In Section 4.2 we derived an expression for $C^S$ (the noble metal concentration in fuel salt) where the fuel loop is considered to be a well stirred pot.
For this assumption to be adequate, the residence time of noble metals in fuel salt must be greater than the circuit time of salt around the fuel loop ( $\sim$ 25 seconds).
\begin{array}{l} X = \lambda + \frac {1}{V} (h ^ {g r a} A ^ {g r a} + h ^ {h e} A ^ {h e} + (h A) ^ {r e s t} o f l o o p \\ + h ^ {b u b} A ^ {b u b}) \\ \end{array}
With the parameters listed in Tables B-1 and B-2, we can compute the time constants involved and they are listed in Table B-5.
In the case of fuel salt with no bubbles and fuel salt during the $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ runs, the well stirred pot assumption is adequate.
It is apparently not adequate during the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ runs.
In Section 5.2 we deduced that the effective sticking fraction to bubbles is considerably less than unity and a value of 0.1 - 0.2 is suggested.
If a sticking fraction to bubbles of 0.1 is assumed, then the noble metal half life in fuel salt during the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ runs becomes 54 seconds.

X (hrs-1)Half Life in Salt (sec)
Fuel Salt with no Bubbles6.7370
Fuel Salt During the 235U Runs31.280
Fuel Salt During the 233U Runs4026.2
